name: Cool Roof Coating
description: High-albedo reflective coating on rooftops to reduce heat absorption. Increases solar reflectance from ~0.15 to 0.60–0.80, significantly reducing indoor and ambient temperatures.
evidence_source: Published urban heat island studies (Akbari et al., 2005; Santamouris et al., 2011). Widely validated in Indian cities by TERI and NRDC.
assumptions: Assumes proper application and maintenance. Cooling effect varies by roof material, age, and local climate. Reapplication needed every 3–5 years.

name: Urban Tree Canopy Expansion
description: Planting native, drought-tolerant shade trees along streets, parks, and public spaces. Provides evapotranspiration cooling and shade, reducing surface and air temperatures.
evidence_source: EPA Heat Island Compendium; Bowler et al. (2010) meta-analysis; IPCC AR6 urban adaptation chapter. Local data from Jodhpur's Rao Jodha Desert Rock Park restoration.
assumptions: Full cooling benefit realized after 5–10 years of growth. Requires sustained irrigation in arid climates. Species selection critical — recommended: Neem (Azadirachta indica), Peepal (Ficus religiosa), Babool (Vachellia nilotica).

name: Shade Structures & Canopies
description: Tensile fabric canopies, pergolas, and shade sails installed over pedestrian corridors, marketplaces, bus stops, and public gathering areas to block direct solar radiation.
evidence_source: Middel et al. (2021) shade intervention studies; Jodhpur Smart City Mission shade pilot evaluations; NIUA heat action plan guidelines.
assumptions: Cooling measured as Mean Radiant Temperature reduction, not air temperature. Localized effect within 2–5 m radius. Requires maintenance and wind-load engineering for desert conditions.

name: Reflective / Cool Pavements
description: Applying high-albedo surface treatments or permeable paving materials to roads, parking areas, and walkways to reduce heat storage and re-radiation from paved surfaces.
evidence_source: Santamouris (2013) cool pavement meta-analysis; US DOE cool pavement field trials; CEPT University Ahmedabad studies on Indian road surfaces.
assumptions: Effectiveness depends on traffic volume (wear) and dust accumulation. Albedo degrades over time. Maintenance coating every 2–4 years. May increase glare — not suitable for all road types.

name: Public Water Misting & Cooling Points
description: Misting fans, drinking water stations, and evaporative cooling shelters placed at high-footfall locations — bus stands, markets, temples, and labour congregation points.
evidence_source: Rajasthan State Disaster Response Authority heat action plans; WHO heat-health guidance; field evaluations from Ahmedabad Heat Action Plan (2013–present).
assumptions: Cooling is hyper-local (3–5 m radius for misting). Water supply must be reliable. Effective primarily during dry heat (low humidity). Not a substitute for systemic cooling — emergency intervention only.

name: Heat Alert & Early Warning System
description: Automated SMS, loudspeaker, and app-based alerts to vulnerable populations when ward-level heat risk exceeds thresholds. Includes recommended protective actions and nearest cooling shelter locations.
evidence_source: Ahmedabad Heat Action Plan mortality reduction data (Hess et al., 2018); NDMA Heat Wave Guidelines (2019); IMD heat alert framework.
assumptions: Does not reduce physical temperature — reduces exposure and mortality through behavioral change. Effectiveness depends on message reach, trust in authorities, and availability of cooling infrastructure. Requires annual maintenance and stakeholder coordination.
